data.title: PagerDuty pops despite mixed Q2 results as new CRO could fuel FY27 growth
data.description:
PagerDuty (NYSE:PD) shares popped up 5% by noon trading on Thursday despite reporting mixed second quarter fiscal 2026 results and slightly lowering its full-year revenue guidance.

The uptick could be due to the digital operations management company announcing the appointment of Todd McNabb as its new chief revenue officer. McNabb previously served as CRO at PROS and ScienceLogic. 

"His unique combination of strategic sales leadership, channel development expertise, and building world-class enterprise organizations aligns well with our mission to revolutionize operations," said PagerDuty CEO Jennifer Tejada.

Canaccord Genuity reiterated its Buy rating but slightly lowered its price target to $19 from $21 following the second quarter results.

"PagerDuty's mixed results, including revenue slightly below the midpoint of guidance underscore the firm's ongoing GTM realignment amidst broad-based seat-based headwinds primarily in the SMB (and more recently in the enterprise)," said Canaccord Genuity analysts Kingsley Crane and Daniel Reagan, in a Thursday investor note. "To that end, today's announcement that Todd McNabb will step in as CRO should be well received by investors."

"Looking ahead, we believe there is a pathway to an upward inflection in growth in FY27 if SMB growth can consistently tick up and large enterprises continue to elevate PD's strategic position in internal IT operations," Crane added. "Improving profit performance is an enduring silver lining in recent quarters, and it's reasonable that PD reaches 25% operating margins on an annual basis within the next 3 years, in our view."

PagerDuty's peers were mixed during Thursday trading. Asana (ASAN) was up 2%, Datadog (DDOG) was down 0.8%, and GitLab (GTLB) had plunged 8%.

data.title: Nuclear energy momentum ‘never seen before’ due to climate change and geopolitics – Urenco CEO
data.description:
Nuclear energy (NYSEARCA:NLR), (NUKZ), (URAN) is experiencing a momentum that has never been seen before, primarily driven by climate change concerns and geopolitical shifts following the Ukraine war, according to Urenco CEO Boris Schucht.

“There is a momentum for nuclear that I must say, as a nuclear engineer and as a businessman, I’ve never seen before,” Schucht said during an interview with CNBC on the sidelines of the World Nuclear Symposium 2025.

Countries like Sweden, Poland, Czech Republic, UK, and France are pursuing new nuclear projects as they recognize that achieving climate targets and securing energy supplies will require nuclear power in their energy mix.

The Ukraine war has created a significant shift in the geopolitical landscape, questioning dependencies on Russia. This change has generated additional demand in the nuclear fuel cycle, prompting new investments to meet these needs, Schucht said.

When asked about AI’s impact on energy demand, the CEO of the multi-national limited company that specializes in uranium enrichment for power generation contextualized nuclear’s role in the broader decarbonization challenge.

While acknowledging that AI creates “additional demand, which we all underestimated a few years ago,” he said that “nuclear is much more a solution for the long term” due to regulatory processes and safety considerations.

Beyond the technology sector, other industries are also looking to nuclear energy for decarbonization solutions.

“There are a lot of other sectors which are thinking about how they decarbonize and how they make sure that they have a clean, affordable energy and safe, reliable energy in the coming years. For example, the chemistry sector,” Schucht said, pointing to growing demand across different industries for nuclear energy as a clean and reliable power source.

The VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NYSEARCA:NLR) has surged 44.4% year-to-date, while the Global X Uranium ETF (NYSEARCA:URA) is up 50.3% year-to-date. Uranium producers, however, has seen a recent decline in their share prices due to an announcement by a joint venture by France's Orano Mining and Kazakhstan's Kazatomprom saying it plans to ramp up uranium production due to falling global uranium prices.

Green Energy ETFs: (ICLN), (TAN), (FAN), (GRID), (QCLN), (ERTH)

data.title: Couchbase Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.02 beats by $0.01, revenue of $57.57M in-line
data.description:
• Couchbase press release (NASDAQ:BASE): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.02 beats by $0.01.
  • Revenue of $57.57M (+11.6% Y/Y) in-line.

data.title: Hudson Global to rebrand as Star Equity Holdings after merger
data.description:
• Hudson Global (NASDAQ:HSON) said on Thursday, it will change its corporate name to Star Equity Holdings Inc, effective September 5, following its August 22 merger with Star Operating Companies Inc.
  • The company’s ticker symbols will also change to STRR (common stock) and STRRP (preferred stock) on NASDAQ, replacing HSON and HSONP.
  • The combined company will be led by Jeff Eberwein as CEO and Rick Coleman as COO.
  • HSON is +0.3% to $9.9.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Microsoft said to be near deal to avoid antitrust fines from EU: report
data.description:
• Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) is nearing a deal with the European Union that would allow the tech giant to stave off antitrust fines, Bloomberg reported. 
  • Regulators from the continent are likely to accept Microsoft's promise to remove Teams from its Office suite of software packages, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. The decision comes after rivals did not raise any concerns in a market test, the people added.
  • In May, it was reported that Microsoft would likely avoid being penalized a hefty amount from the European Union after its latest offer on its Office and Teams product.
  • Microsoft and the European Commission did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Microsoft came under fire when Slack, owned by Salesforce (CRM), complained in 2020 to the European Commission that Microsoft received an unfair advantage by bundling Office and Teams together.

data.title: Genuine Parts Company adds Elliott Management nominees to board
data.description:
Shares of Genuine Parts Company (NYSE:GPC) were briefly launched to an 11-month high after adding new members its corporate board, nominated by activist investor Elliott Management.

"As one of GPC's largest investors, we believe the new additions to the Board and the ongoing strategic and operational review represent critical steps toward ensuring that GPC reaches its full potential. We believe the company's current share price does not reflect the true value of its automotive aftermarket and industrial distribution businesses, and that there is a clear path to creating substantial, long-term value at GPC,” Elliott’s Marc Steinberg said.

To accommodate Elliott’s efforts to improve the company’s profitability and value, Genuine Parts added former TricorBraun CEO Courtney Carruthers and former Home Depot (HD) CIO Matt Carey to the board, replacing retiring members Robert Loudermilk, Jr., and John Holder.

The board changes were made in connection with a Cooperation Agreement between Genuine Parts (NYSE:GPC) and Elliott Management, which includes an information-sharing agreement that will allow for ongoing dialogue between the two parties.

data.title: Shoe Carnival raises annual EPS guidance and targets majority Shoe Station fleet by 2026 as rebanner strategy drives margin expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Shoe Carnival, Inc. (SCVL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Mark Worden, President and CEO, highlighted that "Our second quarter results demonstrate meaningful progress on our corporate strategy. We beat earnings consensus by over 20% and expanded gross margins 270 basis points to 38.8%, our strongest Q2 margin in years." He emphasized the company's rebanner strategy, stating, "Shoe Station outperformed Shoe Carnival by over 10% on merchandise sales during Q2 and back-to-school." Worden noted a demographic shift as Shoe Station attracts households with over $50,000 in income, reducing exposure to economic downturns and driving improved economics across the portfolio.
  • Worden announced, "All of this delivered $0.70 in EPS, beating expectations and giving us the confidence to raise our annual profit guidance range today." He shared ambitious store growth targets: "By the end of fiscal 2025, we'll operate 145 Shoe Station stores, approximately 1/3 of our entire fleet. By back-to-school 2026, we'll surpass 215 stores, 51% of the current fleet at Shoe Stations."
  • Addressing liquidity, Worden said, "As of fiscal August end, cash and securities are up double digits year-over-year at nearly $150 million, debt is zero."
  • Patrick Edwards, Senior VP, CFO, stated, "Second quarter net sales were $306.4 million compared to $332.7 million in the prior year. The 7.9% change reflects our strategic focus on higher-margin business as we transform our customer mix and banner portfolio."

Outlook

  • Edwards provided updated guidance: "Net sales guidance is now $1.12 billion to $1.15 billion, tightened from our previous range." He also raised the EPS guidance range: "We're raising the EPS guidance range from $1.70 to $2.10, increasing the low end by $0.10."
  • Gross profit margin guidance was increased: "Gross profit margin guidance increases 150 basis points to 36.5% to 37.5%, reflecting the structural margin improvement from rebanners and disciplined pricing."
  • For Q3, Edwards shared, "We expect net sales of $290 million to $300 million and EPS of $0.50 to $0.55."

Financial Results

  • Shoe Carnival reported Q2 net sales of $306.4 million and comparable store sales decline of 7.5%, including approximately 100 basis points of impact from 20 completed rebanners.
  • Shoe Station sales grew 1.6% with essentially flat comparable store sales, while Shoe Carnival sales declined 10.1%. Rogan delivered approximately $20 million in net sales.
  • Gross profit margin reached 38.8%, a 270 basis point expansion versus last year. Merchandise margins improved 390 basis points, driven by disciplined pricing, favorable mix, and strategic inventory investments. SG&A expenses were $93.6 million or 30.6% of sales.
  • Net income was $19.2 million or $0.70 per diluted share compared to $22.6 million or $0.82 last year. Cash and marketable securities totaled $91.9 million at quarter end, rising to over $148 million following August's performance. Inventory at quarter end was $449 million, up 5% from last year.

Q&A

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research Partners: Asked if there was a change in priorities to achieve the margin results. Worden responded, "The opportunistic buys and additional inventory that the team brought in performed better than we expected...Shoe Station performance continues to accelerate."
  • Kummetz inquired about Q3 comp expectations and margins. Edwards answered, "The $290 million to $300 million range that we've given is down 2 to down 5...With respect to margin, we earned 36% in the quarter last year. We would expect a number that is 100 to 150 basis points above that in Q3 this year."
  • Kummetz asked about managing Shoe Carnival. Worden explained, "We're seeing the competitive set go after that low-income strap household with very aggressive pricing activity...we're strategically moving away from that sub-$30,000 households."
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: Questioned inventory levels and gross margin guidance. Tanya Gordon, Chief Merchandising Officer, said, "Inventory at the end of August...is really in line with where we ended Q2, up mid-singles. We strategically went after inventory to bill for back-to-school, which helped us deliver that comp growth in the month of August."
  • Poser asked about brand mix performance and future Jordan products. Worden replied, "We're not going to share with our competitors what new products are coming in...Our higher ticket items, best brands in the world...have been a winning recipe for us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on inventory levels, margin sustainability, and the impact of shifting demographics, showing a slightly skeptical tone regarding inventory risks and future margin maintenance.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone, frequently referencing disciplined strategy and positive results. Phrases such as "we believe that's not the strategy for us" and "we have great confidence" were used.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence appeared to strengthen, especially in highlighting the rebanner strategy's success and the improving financial position.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised annual EPS guidance (previously $1.60 to $2.10, now $1.70 to $2.10) and tightened net sales guidance (now $1.12 billion to $1.15 billion from $1.15 billion to $1.23 billion).
  • Gross margin guidance moved higher (now 36.5% to 37.5%, up from 35% to 36%).
  • Store conversion targets accelerated, with Shoe Station expected to represent 51% of the fleet by back-to-school 2026, up from 28% projected previously for the end of fiscal 2025.
  • Management tone shifted from "cautiously optimistic" in Q1 to more overtly confident in Q2, citing strong execution and margin expansion.
  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent on inventory, banner performance, and margin sustainability, but the Q2 dialogue showed increased scrutiny on inventory risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged, "We have too much [inventory]...we have it in places we feel good about delivering strong margins as we work through the fall season." They expect to normalize inventory levels in 2026, with timing dependent on tariffs and supply chain clarity.
  • Analysts raised concerns about inventory levels, margin erosion, and the impact of tariffs on vendor pricing. Gordon noted, "We're looking at price increases between 5% and 7% in total based on what we've gotten back thus far."
  • Worden stated, "We do not see that margin erosion becoming relevant in this fiscal year, and we do not see that product being margin deteriorating next year."

Final Takeaway

Shoe Carnival’s Q2 2025 call showcased accelerated progress on its rebanner strategy, with Shoe Station driving growth in higher-margin, premium segments and setting a new trajectory for the company’s demographic mix. Management raised annual EPS and gross margin guidance, validated by a strong back-to-school season and robust cash position. Inventory remains elevated by design, supporting in-stock rates and margin expansion, and the company expects to reach a critical mass with Shoe Station representing the majority of its store fleet by back-to-school 2026, marking a pivotal shift in business strategy and financial outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street trades higher ahead of Friday's payrolls report
data.description:
Wall Street trades higher on Thursday as investors weigh fresh jobs data for clues on the labor market’s strength.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is +0.3%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is +0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) is +0.4%.

On a sector-by-sector basis, eight of the 11 S&P segments are trading in positive territory, led by Consumer Discretionary and Energy. At the other end of the spectrum, Utilities has lagged the most on the session. 

In the bond market, yields are under some slight pressure as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) has dipped 2 basis points to 4.19%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) had pushed down 2 basis points to 3.59%.

On the economic front, job cuts climbed 39% in August, boosting the YTD total higher than all of 2024. Also, U.S. private sector employment continued softening, slightly more than expected, in August. 

Initial jobless claims rose slightly more than expected in the past week. Productivity was revised up, and labor costs were revised lower in second Q2 prints. 

The U.S. goods and services trade deficit widened more than expected in July.

Additionally, the U.S. ISM Services Index rose to 52.0 in August from 50.1 in July, exceeding the 50.9 consensus, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management

"The global bond selloff finally paused for breath yesterday, as weak U.S. data meant investors ramped up their expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year. The main catalyst was the JOLTS report for July, which showed that job openings fell to a 10-month low and exacerbated fears about a labor market slowdown," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

On more of the political side, President Donald Trump on Wednesday urged the Supreme Court to quickly accept and rule on an appeal aiming to overturn lower court rulings that deemed most of his tariffs illegal.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) have surged 32.1% after the apparel maker said the advertising campaign starring actress Sydney Sweeney gave its brand and business a reset.

data.title: Elevance falls on softer margin estimate, reaffirmed 2025 guidance
data.description:
Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV) shares fell on Thursday after the company's executive said it does not expect improvements in its operational Medicaid margins for the second half of the year.

At the Wells Fargo Healthcare conference, an executive of the company reflected on the challenging environment in the market, and said that its operating margin performance is expected to be softer than the outlook that provided in July.

The company, through a filing, also said it expects to reaffirm earnings guidance for full year 2025 at about $24.10 per share, including $5.90 per share of net unfavorable items.

Elevance also expects to reaffirm 2025 benefit expense ratio guidance of about 90%.

The shares of the company are down about 4%. Peers Centene (CNC) and Molina Healthcare (MOH) are also down about 5% today.

data.title: Trump Fed nominee Miran says he'll preserve central bank independence
data.description:
Stephen Miran, President Donald Trump's nominee for a Federal Reserve Board governor, said on Thursday he'll act independently based on his assessment of economic data and emphasized the importance of an independent central bank.

"The Federal Open Market Committee is an independent group with a monumental task, and I intend to preserve that independence and serve the American people to the best of my ability," he said in his confirmation hearing in the U.S. Senate.

"In my view, the most important job of the central bank is to prevent Depressions and hyperinflations," he said in his prepared opening remarks for the hearing. "Independence of monetary policy is a critical element for its success."

"It's important to hear a variety of views," he added in questioning by Senator Tim Scott (R-SC).

He sees the need for a full review of the Fed's regulatory function, he added. Miran also said he'll push back on the central bank straying from its mandate. For example, he considers the Fed's actions regarding climate change to be highly political.

The nominee declined to say whether he believed the Bureau of Labor Statistics' jobs data was "faked," but repeated several times that he believes the quality of various measures have steadily deteriorated.

He also refused to give a "yes" or "no" answer on whether Donald Trump lost the 2020 election. Instead, he responded that Congress certified the election of Joe Biden as president.

Miran said he'll take an unpaid leave from his current role heading the Council of Economic Advisers. He was nominated to fill the vacancy on the Fed board created when Adriana Kugler resigned in August. Her term ends in January 2026. If he's nominated and confirmed for a longer term, he'll resign from the council, he said.

Senator Jack Reed (D-RI) countered that if Miran serves on the Fed while on leave from the CEA, he'll be a White House employee who would also be serving on the Fed.

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
